ed_at":"2024-12-03T00:02:30Z","updated_at":"2024-12-06T08:21:33Z","closed_at":"2024-12-03T14:54:06Z","author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27891","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27891","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27891.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27891.patch","merged_at":"2024-12-03T14:54:06Z"},"body":"This is a follow-up PR after https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27716. It updates `np.random` functions and methods' `__module__` to `numpy.random`.

The only items left with incorrect `__module__` are cdef classes, as Cython doesn't allows changing it.

List from here: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/136559#issuecomment-2420637016","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27891/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27891/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2522489849","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27891#issuecomment-2522489849","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27891","id":2522489849,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86WWiP5","user":{"login":"da-woods","id":10536947,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNTM2OTQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10536947?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods","html_url":"https://github.com/da-woods","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-06T08:21:31Z","updated_at":"2024-12-06T08:21:31Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Re: module for cdef classes. A good fix looks really hard. The basic problem is that `type` defines its own property for `__module__`  which ignores whatever you set in the class dictionary for static types.

However. For heap types it works:

```
# distutils: extra_compile_args = -DCYTHON_USE_TYPE_SPECS=1

cdef class C:
    __module__ = "a"

print(C.__module__)  # prints "a"
```

Is that any use to Numpy?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2522489849/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

That seem silly but it's we don't have another good way of doing it and it's pretty necessary to get useful tracebacks out of Cython code.

In a real emergency we could could resort to the limited API fallback code instead. I'd rather not because it makes exceptions really slow, but it's an option if nothing else works.

For a wild guess at using that as a short-term solution you can define the C macro `CYTHON_LIMITED_API=1` (but _not_ `Py_LIMITED_API`). I don't see anything in `_specfunc.pyx` to make me think that won't work, but I haven't tried it myself.

### Reproduce the code example:

Put the following into ./Dockerfile:

```dockerfile
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1.3

FROM python:3.14.0a1-bookworm

RUN pip install cython meson-python ninja
RUN pip install numpy==2.1.2
```

and run `docker build .`

Introduction
Rebase, reorganization, and code review of [PR 17019](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/17019).

#### Original description
Another pull request to speed up sparse.csgraph.dijkstra, succeeding https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/16696
Thank you @jjerphan for reviewing the previous pull request and giving me advice

* Replaces FibonacciHeap with std::priority_queue which makes sparse.csgraph.dijkstra faster
   * asymptotically due to the removal of a bug in FibonacciHeap implementation
   * with regard to the constant factor due to better memory usage(?)
* Unifies the four similar dijkstra functions into one
   * Performance overhead was negligible, probably because the priority_queue is the bottleneck
* Adds the star graph to the benchmark as the worst case for the previous version
* Fixes a typo in the test file name
* A part of the test file for dijkstra is modified for better readability, nothing really changed
( See benchmarks in [PR 17019](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/17019)).

### Changes from original PR
* Squashed tiny commits into larger commits (e.g., [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/17019/commits/90b5151b766f8d68c74fa0f8b5ca6ecd62eb7bf5)).
* Removing irrelevant commits due to changes in repo since the original PR was opened ( e.g., [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/17019/commits/92b53181f1fcdb90ec8d3ef2285195e2271c2d55) one).
* Adapted Yen's algorithm to the new Dijkstra internal implementation (under [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/commit/24fddcd7f7d0e8bf65724583515f86361266e7ff) commit).
* Removed random testing ( as I agree with the comment [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/17800#pullrequestreview-1251442515)), instead added a star-graph test.

Just to confirm, that's:

>   /home/runner/work/scipy/scipy/scipy/sparse/csgraph/_shortest_path.pyx:713:25: C++ class must have a nullary constructor to be stack allocated

????

I'd suspect that's a bug in the current Cython master vs whatever Cython release you're using for the rest of the builds. I doubt it's related to free-threading particularly. It is indeed a slight behaviour change from 3.0 -> master.

Cython has some slightly different behaviour depending on if it believes it's compiling in C++ mode or in C mode. Specifically to do with having multiple definitions of the same function.

This is controlled by the flag `--cplus` to Cython, or in the `distutils/setuptools` world the `language` argument to "extensions". You've moved beyond the `distutils/setuptools` world so I don't know quite what's going on, but you aren't passing the flag `--cplus` for this file.

The upshot is that Cython believes its operating in C mode so doesn't apply the extra C++ rules.

On the Cython side I'm going to make a small modification so that it always treats `cppclass` as C++ (whatever it thinks about the file as a whole). I think that makes sense and would stop this little bit of breakage.

From your side I think you should be passing the `--cplus` flag to Cython (at least for everything that uses C++). I don't know how you do it in your build system, but hopefully you know and this is enough information to point you in the right direction.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2156637336/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```
def inc1_cfloat_struct(np.ndarray[np.cfloat_t] arr):
    arr[1].real += 1
    arr[1].imag += 1
```

into this C code (cleaned up and simplified a bit from the actual code)
```
(* (npy_cfloat *)__pyx_pybuffernd_arr.rcbuffer->pybuffer.buf + 1)).imag += 1;
```

but `npy_cfloat` on NumPy2.0 no longer is a struct with `real` and `imag` fields. See https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/6076#issuecomment-1998113565. Can we give easy left-hand access to the `imag` part of a complex type? The [migration guide](https://numpy.org/devdocs/numpy_2_0_migration_guide.html#complex-types-underlying-type-changes) mentions `npy_csetreal` and `npy_csetimag`, but then the codegen will need to do something very different.

cc @lysnikolaou

Fixes https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/28530

Relates to cython/cython#2747

Fedora bug: https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2261602

#### What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.

This problem was mentioned in a bug open to keep track of the effort to remove all compilation warnings #24875 (search "Incompatible pointers types casts")

With gcc < 14 the code compiles with incompatible-pointer warning. But with gcc 14 that warning is an error.

A possible workaround is to remove the inline in the definition of the `rdist_csr` and `rdist` members.

With that change, cython generates correct code (I don't know why). But removing the inline can impact the performance.

base"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2023-12-08T12:02:33Z","updated_at":"2023-12-16T18:04:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"As part of the Cythonization efforts, I have been hitting the common issue of getting access to complex versions of abs, sqrt, log and so on functions. This is problematic for a few reasons, 

- MSVC in windows is c99 incompatible and we all know the history etc. it is what it is
- npy_math is on the pedestal with discussions such as https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/20880 and similar discussions elsewhere hence it is only going to be less useful in the future, in my limited understanding
- Cython injects guard macros after the includes and we have to play with the `CCYTHON_COMPLEX` env var and do some custom bricklaying at the Cython compilation setup (if I am understanding correctly) Here is a better discussion of it https://stackoverflow.com/questions/57837255/defining-dcomplex-externally-in-cython

In our legacy code we have a few avenues of "hacks", namely 

- the types switching back and forth between `_DComplex` , `double complex` and `npy_cdouble` types in unions or casting schemes, 
- The imports from `npy_math` as in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/fa9f13e6906e7d00510d593f7f982db30e4e4f14/scipy/special/_complexstuff.pxd#L12-L24
- and some other redefinition clauses of `_Dcomplex` and so on

None of them seems to be the clean way but I'd like to ask our build residents what way we should pursue to have a sustainable code? 

I'll ping Cython, NumPy folks too, so that we don't stray too outwards @da-woods @lysnikolaou @rgommers @seberg

I would really appreciate a mini code block that should run succesfully on a win or *nix system such as 


```python
%%cython -f
import numpy as np
cimport numpy as cnp

# just to make sure that things real and complex funcs can coexist
from libc.math cimport (abs, sqrt)

<insert complex cimport magic here>


cdef double test_func(double complex z):
    cdef double absz, sqaz
    absz = cabs(z)
    sqaz = sqrt(absz)
    return sqaz
```

If you want to make functions run with `npy_cdouble` and `npy_cabs` that's also fine. I just need some clarification which way we should be going towards. 

We can also disallow MSVC but I feel that it's a bit too much of a restriction.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/19657/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/19657/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/1858889725","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/19657#issuecomment-1858889725","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/19657","id":1858889725,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc5uzGf9","user":{"login":"da-woods","id":10536947,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNTM2OTQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10536947?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods","html_url":"https://github.com/da-woods","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-12-16T18:04:14Z","updated_at":"2023-12-16T18:04:14Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I haven't played too much with complex in *real* code, so a little unclear on the issues (although I totally believe they exist!)

With recent Cython (3.0+) on Linux I just have to add `from libc.complex cimport cabs` and your code compiles for me as written on Linux with GCC. My impression is that `CYTHON_CCOMPLEX` gets detected mostly correctly, and so the `complex double` types can get passed to suitable C functions. I imagine you don't have to go that far back for that not to be true though.

The alternative Numpy `npy_cdouble` types don't look like they'll automatically work though.

So it looks like the basic problem is: you've got a bunch of types that are very close to equivalent (same information, same memory layout, but different name). And perhaps what would be useful to do is have a way of declaring "this is a complex type" in Cython. Maybe assume that they're all compatible with the basic C or C++ standard library memory layout (which I think is true) and let Cython do the automatic conversions between them.

That's probably something we could support better in some future version of Cython, although doesn't help now.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/1858889725/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

_apply -> _add_datetime and type it as requiring/returning a datetime object.

This currently fails on TestBusinessDay::test_apply_corner where it raises RecursionError instead of TypeError.  I could use some help figuring out why (cc @WillAyd?)  Roughly:

```
off = pd.offsets.BDay()
other = pd.BMonthEnd()
with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=msg):
>    off + other

pandas/tests/tseries/offsets/test_business_day.py:237: 
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ 
offsets.pyx:1715: in pandas._libs.tslibs.offsets.BusinessDay.__add__
    ???
offsets.pyx:495: in pandas._libs.tslibs.offsets.BaseOffset.__radd__
    ???
offsets.pyx:495: in pandas._libs.tslibs.offsets.BaseOffset.__radd__
    ???
E   RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded while calling a Python object
!!! Recursion detected (same locals & position)
```

IIUC `BusinessDay.__add__` is (correctly) returning NotImplemented, so `BMonthEnd.__radd__` is called, which also returns NotImplemented.  But instead of python then raising TypeError, it looks like it just keeps calling `__radd__`. ```
cdef class C:
    def __add__(self, other):
        return NotImplemented

    def __radd__(self, other):
        return self.__add__(other)
```

I think (but haven't rigorously verified) that the problem is that we don't define `__add__` in the class dictionary. This means that when you do `self.__add__`, it doesn't call the `__add__` function as written, but instead calls the dispatch function that against tries `__radd__`.

I'll create a Cython bug report. If it's what I've suggest above then it probably isn't too hard to fix.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/comments/1824819116/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

to https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/27086 but specialized to the regression observed in `HistGradientBoostingClassifier` for better tracking.

With cython 3, it looks that the speed of 2 parts has changed.
- the initial binning is faster, which is a good thing
- the computation of the histograms at each iteration is slower which is a bad thing.

Here's a profiling that clearly shows that the method `HistogramBuilder.compute_histograms_brute` is a lot slower than it used to be

**cython 0.29.36**
![prof_hgbt_cy2](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/assets/34657725/c2785724-52fc-4060-8a16-ec0a57e4f46c)

**cython 3.0.2 master**
![prof_hgbt_cy3](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/assets/34657725/9828cf43-7859-4653-a2bd-f618d9422326)

Looking at the cython annotated version of this function I can see that the generated code is different in both cases (~50% more lines of code with cython 3) but I've no clue how to interpret it. Below is the annotated version just for the `def` part of the following method
![Screenshot 2023-09-20 at 16-12-06 Cython histogram pyx](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/assets/34657725/da1495fd-fe7a-40de-b591-2d58549b2121)

**cython 0.29.36**

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/19404","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/19404/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/19404/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/19404/events","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/19404","id":1949977830,"node_id":"I_kwDOABZIoc50Okzm","number":19404,"title":"RFC: seeking thoughts on rewriting special functions written in Cython into C++","user":{"login":"steppi","id":1953382,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5NTMzODI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1953382?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/steppi","html_url":"https://github.com/steppi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/steppi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/steppi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/steppi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/steppi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/steppi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/steppi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/steppi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/steppi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/steppi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":37340790,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MDc5MA==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/scipy.special","name":"scipy.special","color":"0366d6","default":false,"description":""},{"id":5432239077,"node_id":"LA_kwDOABZIoc8AAAABQ8lj5Q","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/RFC","name":"RFC","color":"FEF2C0","default":false,"description":"Request for Comments; typically used to gather feedback for a substantial change proposal"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":24,"created_at":"2023-10-18T15:04:43Z","updated_at":"2023-10-19T20:29:00Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"At the moment we have a number of functions in `special` where we have provided our own implementation using Cython. I think Cython was chosen for its ergonomics and because it may be easier to maintain for developers used to Python. Its use comes with some downsides though, and using C++ brings upsides.

@izaid has pointed out that if we can supply C++ implementations in header files, than other array computing libraries such as CuPy, PyTorch, or Jax could potentially use these implementations. https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/19023#issuecomment-1689050946. @mdhaber, izaid and myself have been discussing proposing an array API extension for special functions, and moving to C++ could help facilitate that. 

Lack of interoperability affects us internally too. Because of how the build system works currently for special functions, the compiled kernels for special functions written in Cython can not be called from compiled kernels written in C or C++. This is blocking https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/19287 at the moment, cc @rlucas7.

Since the special function infrastructure already handles generating the ufunc boilerplate on the NumPy C API side, we don't get the primary benefit of using Cython in special. Where Cython really shines is in writing extensions without needing to work out the somewhat tedious C API boilerplate.

@izaid has generously offered to help review PRs I submit for rewriting special functions from Cython into C++, and I would be happy to review any PRs he submits. We would prioritize functions that we think should be included in an array API extension.

My thought here is that we should try to write these in the style of Boost special functions, making use of Boost's fundamental building blocks when possible, with the idea that perhaps we could eventually contribute these upstream to Boost. At the moment, the biggest thing missing from Boost is complex support for special functions where that is relevant. cc @mborland. This is the approach @tirthasheshpatel and I are looking at for our SDG to work on special infrastructure; making use of Boost's internal building blocks, and writing our own building blocks in a similar style if needed.

As part of this, we would also avoid writing any new special functions in Cython, recommending instead C++ in the development docs.

There were a few comments about complex and MSVC - I think they're mostly related to C (where it does something very non-standard). I think for C++ it follows the standard. Recent MSVC has pretty good conformance with the C++ standard in my experience.

----------------------

> Since the special function infrastructure already handles generating the ufunc boilerplate on the NumPy C API side, we don't get the primary benefit of using Cython in special. Where Cython really shines is in writing extensions without needing to work out the somewhat tedious C API boilerplate.

Cython can generate ufunc boilerplate for you now (probably not relevant for this discussion though)

> C does not support simple overloading on argument types, C++ does

This does make it hard of you want to provide an `extern "C"` interface (since such an interface doesn't allow overloading). It doesn't sound like that's a big concern right now.

-------------------

As @matusvalo and @h-vetinari say: you can get Cython to generate a C header file with `cdef public`. As @rgommers notes - that generates an extension module that you need to import (i.e. it isn't just a stand-alone C/C++ library). That probably makes it less useful for people that want to use them from C or C++.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/1771659764/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

to https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/27086 but specialized to the regression observed in `HistGradientBoostingClassifier` for better tracking.

With cython 3, it looks that the speed of 2 parts has changed.
- the initial binning is faster, which is a good thing
- the computation of the histograms at each iteration is slower which is a bad thing.

Here's a profiling that clearly shows that the method `HistogramBuilder.compute_histograms_brute` is a lot slower than it used to be

**cython 0.29.36**
![prof_hgbt_cy2](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/assets/34657725/c2785724-52fc-4060-8a16-ec0a57e4f46c)

**cython 3.0.2 master**
![prof_hgbt_cy3](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/assets/34657725/9828cf43-7859-4653-a2bd-f618d9422326)

Looking at the cython annotated version of this function I can see that the generated code is different in both cases (~50% more lines of code with cython 3) but I've no clue how to interpret it. Below is the annotated version just for the `def` part of the following method
![Screenshot 2023-09-20 at 16-12-06 Cython histogram pyx](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/assets/34657725/da1495fd-fe7a-40de-b591-2d58549b2121)

**cython 0.29.36**
![Screenshot 2023-09-20 at 16-07-50 Cython histogram 2 pyx](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/assets/34657725/989c80dd-95f9-40e1-b8d7-efa3d69bc96a)

**cython 3.0.2 master**
![Screenshot 2023-09-20 at 16-08-47 Cython histogram 3 pyx](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/assets/34657725/1dd6b58c-3444-4d70-855e-04cb58ee333c)

I did not find any difference in the body of the method.

Ping @da-woods in case you have some time to take a look :)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/27426/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/27426/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/comments/1728061562","html_url":"https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/27426#issuecomment-1728061562","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/27426","id":1728061562,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAzd1s5nACB6","user":{"login":"da-woods","id":10536947,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNTM2OTQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10536947?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods","html_url":"https://github.com/da-woods","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-09-20T16:20:23Z","updated_at":"2023-09-20T16:20:23Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"My guess (without yet having had a look at the generated code) would be that this is probably in `_compute_histogram_brute_single_feature` and would be fixed in https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/5685. I'm running

```
import numpy as np
from histogram import _build_histogram, HistogramBuilder
import time

HISTOGRAM_DTYPE = np.dtype([
    ('sum_gradients', np.float64),  # sum of sample gradients in bin
    ('sum_hessians', np.float64),  # sum of sample hessians in bin
    ('count', np.uint32),  # number of samples in bin
])

rng = np.random.RandomState(42)
n_sub_samples = 1000000
n_samples = 10000000
n_bins = 256

binned_feature = rng.randint(0, n_bins - 1, size=(n_samples, 20), dtype=np.uint8)
binned_feature = np.asfortranarray(binned_feature)
sample_indices = rng.choice(
    np.arange(n_samples, dtype=np.uint32), n_sub_samples, replace=False
)
ordered_gradients = rng.randn(n_sub_samples).astype(np.float64)
ordered_hessians = rng.randn(n_sub_samples).astype(np.float64)

hist = np.zeros((1, n_bins), dtype=HISTOGRAM_DTYPE)
builder = HistogramBuilder(
            binned_feature,
            n_bins,
            ordered_gradients,
            ordered_hessians,
            True,
            4,  # nthreads
        )
t0 = time.time()
histograms = builder.compute_histograms_brute(sample_indices)
t1 = time.time()
print(t1-t0)
```

And I'm getting ~0.38s for Cython master, 3.02, and 0.29.x.

The one thing that may be significant - I took histogram.pyx out of scikit learn and just compiled it myself with `python3 cythonize.py -3if histogram.pyx` (copying a few definitions out of common.pyx). That was just for my convenience.  I added the following directives at the top of the file to match what I think your global build system does:

```
# cython: boundscheck=False
# cython: wraparound=False
# cython: initializedcheck=False
# cython: nonecheck=False
# cython: cdivision=True

Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

One of the the Cython 3 changes was to make cdef functions capable of returning exceptions by default. This has safety benefits, but performance implications in places (especially for functions that end up implicitly `except*` called within a `nogil` block, since they always have to reacquire the GIL to check the exception state - I'm aiming to add some better diagnostic messages for this in Cython in the near future to help users out here).

We've been looking at one such issue https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/5564, and in their particular case it looks like they're hitting it for some of the `scipy.special.cython_special` functions to which to be fused functions. One of the translated lines of C code is

```
__pyx_t_8 = __pyx_fuse_1__pyx_f_5scipy_7special_14cython_special_psi((__pyx_v_G_sh[(__pyx_v_uid_st + __pyx_v_j)]), 0); if (unlikely(__Pyx_ErrOccurredWithGIL())) __PYX_ERR(0, 569, __pyx_L6_error)
```

so they're trying to use `psi`.

I wasn't able to track down the declaration of `psi` easily myself though hence the report is a little vague.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

This should be fixable by adding `noexcept` to the end of the function declaration to indicate that it definitely doesn't raise an exception. `noexcept` is compatible with recent Cython 0.29.x releases (0.29.31 upwards) as a no-op since it's the default there.

If you don't want to break support for older Cython 0.29.x releases you could use a version-specific pxd file by calling it `<something>.cython-30.pxd`

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

_No response_

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->

The `noexcept` marker was introduced by 50d0825, however the docs have not been updated. I found my cython code got broken after updating scipy, complaining about `Cannot assign type 'double (double, void *) except? -1' to 'callback_type'`. The issue was solved after I added `noexcept` to the callback functions.

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->

Just a simple update of the docs.

<!--#### Additional information-->
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->

I've tested with

```
from scipy.optimize.cython_optimize cimport bisect

cdef double callback_func1(double x, void* ignore):
    return x

cdef double callback_func2(double x, void* ignore) noexcept:
    return x


print(bisect(callback_func1, -1, 1, NULL, 1e-6, 1e-6, 500, NULL))
print(bisect(callback_func2, -1, 1, NULL, 1e-6, 1e-6, 500, NULL))
```

(just compiling with `cythonize -if tstcb.pyx`, Cython 0.29.36). Both work.

This will be important for Cython 3 though so it's probably worth updating the docs.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/1666891791/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

improvement"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2023-08-02T07:22:01Z","updated_at":"2023-08-06T15:13:26Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

One of the the Cython 3 changes was to make cdef functions capable of returning exceptions by default. This has safety benefits, but performance implications in places (especially for functions that end up implicitly `except*` called within a `nogil` block, since they always have to reacquire the GIL to check the exception state - I'm aiming to add some better diagnostic messages for this in Cython in the near future to help users out here).

We've been looking at one such issue https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/5564, and in their particular case it looks like they're hitting it for some of the `scipy.special.cython_special` functions to which to be fused functions. One of the translated lines of C code is

```
__pyx_t_8 = __pyx_fuse_1__pyx_f_5scipy_7special_14cython_special_psi((__pyx_v_G_sh[(__pyx_v_uid_st + __pyx_v_j)]), 0); if (unlikely(__Pyx_ErrOccurredWithGIL())) __PYX_ERR(0, 569, __pyx_L6_error)
```

so they're trying to use `psi`.

I wasn't able to track down the declaration of `psi` easily myself though hence the report is a little vague.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

This should be fixable by adding `noexcept` to the end of the function declaration to indicate that it definitely doesn't raise an exception. `noexcept` is compatible with recent Cython 0.29.x releases (0.29.31 upwards) as a no-op since it's the default there.

If you don't want to break support for older Cython 0.29.x releases you could use a version-specific pxd file by calling it `<something>.cython-30.pxd`

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

_No response_

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

A `noexcept` addition in `cython_optimize.pxd` was just reported as a regression in 1.11.0 by @h3jia in [gh-18997](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/18997).

Right... looking in a bit more detail.

In general Cython lets you assign like:

```
cdef void func() noexcept:
   # ...

ctypedef (*fptr)() except *

cdef fptr xx = func
```

So you're able to initialize a function pointer with an exception check from a function without an exception check, and the worst case is you have an unnecessary check. However you're not allowed to do the opposite (because you're missing a check).

This means that adding `noexcept` to the function declarations in `scipy.special.cython_special` should be safe I think.

Where https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/18997 is that the *function pointer types* for callbacks have changed. This *is* potentially an API break since you're assigning to something more restrictive. However I believe it only changes the API in Cython 3 (see the minimal example I posted on that issue).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/1666892102/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Reference Issues/PRs

#### What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.
This PR updates the usage of `const` throughout the `{Ball, KD}Tree` classes to more accurately reflect usage, and to mitigate permissive changes.

#### Any other comments?
I set the language to `c++` to more easily see where permissive casts were being performed. Switching the language on these files to `c++` is **not necessary**, and will be reverted once the PR is closer to being complete.

Currently, tests are failing due to the change in `readonly` behavior since now `idx_array` is _no longer `const`_. However, I believe it needs to be non-`const` since `partition_nodes` uses `std::nth_element` on the index, and `std::nth_element` explicitly reorders the memory afaik.

I'm not sure if there's something else going on, since if my understanding is correct then we really shouldn't have been able to get away with this in the first place, right?

cc: @jjerphan","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/27125/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/27125/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/comments/1687611719","html_url":"https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/27125#issuecomment-1687611719","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/27125","id":1687611719,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAzd1s5klulH","user":{"login":"da-woods","id":10536947,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNTM2OTQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10536947?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods","html_url":"https://github.com/da-woods","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-08-22T07:17:52Z","updated_at":"2023-08-22T07:17:52Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"So if I understand right, the "inconsistency" is that:

```
def f(const double[::1] x):
    cdef double *xptr = &x[0]
```

compiles correctly in Cython. It generates a C warning `warning: assignment discards ‘const’ qualifier from pointer target type` (on gcc at least) but Cython itself doesn't complain.

I definitely agree that this is a bug.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/comments/1687611719/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

On pandas, we updated to Cython 3 but then reverted, since we had issues with some of our benchmarks regressing, and it would be nice to know if this is a bigger problem in Cython.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/27086/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/27086/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/comments/1699677400","html_url":"https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/27086#issuecomment-1699677400","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/27086","id":1699677400,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAzd1s5lTwTY","user":{"login":"da-woods","id":10536947,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNTM2OTQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10536947?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods","html_url":"https://github.com/da-woods","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-08-30T18:51:45Z","updated_at":"2023-08-30T18:51:45Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"General comments - haven't looked into your specific regressions:

The most common issue I've seen is due to the switch from function being implicitly `noexcept` to being able to propagate exceptions by default. Specifically for functions returning `void` this means that there will *always* be an exception check. In a nogil (often parallel) block this means that  they always have to acquire the GIL.

This also applies to functions returning a fused type for slightly more complicated reasons (but the same basic idea).

I'm working on a) a fix for the fused return type and b) better diagnostics of this issue (i.e. to point out places where it might be an issue). Hopefully those will make it into the next release.

Hopefully that gives you a clue where to start looking. If you want I can try to find time to investigate myself (but you'll probably need to give me idiot-proof instructions on how to run the benchmark)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/comments/1699677400/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

The choices are:
* Declare the functions causing the issue as `noexcept`,
* Use the `legacy_implicit_noexcept` compiler directive - I don't really recommend because it's a catch all, but I'll give you a quick diagnostic about if this is the issue,
* Use a dummy `int` return type (and just return 0 on success), this lets Cython use `-1` as an error return type, so you get the benefit of the improved exception propagation with much less performance cost.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/comments/1699793120/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

+.. _cython:
+
+Cython Best Practices, Conventions and Knowledge
+================================================
+
+This documents tips to develop Cython code in scikit-learn.
+
+Tips for developing with Cython in scikit-learn
+-----------------------------------------------
+
+Tips to ease development
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+* Time spent reading `Cython's documentation <https://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/>`_ is not time lost.
+
+* If you intend to use OpenMP: On MacOS, system's distribution of ``clang`` does not implement OpenMP.
+  You can install the ``compilers`` package available on ``conda-forge`` which comes with an implementation of OpenMP.
+
+* Activating `checks <https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/62a017efa047e9581ae7df8bbaa62cf4c0544ee4/sklearn/_build_utils/__init__.py#L68-L87>`_ might help. E.g. for activating boundscheck use:
+
+  .. code-block:: bash
+
+         export SKLEARN_ENABLE_DEBUG_CYTHON_DIRECTIVES=1
+
+* `Start from scratch in a notebook <https://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/src/quickstart/build.html#using-the-jupyter-notebook>`_ to understand how to use Cython and to get feedback on your work quickly.
+  If you plan to use OpenMP for your implementations in your Jupyter Notebook, do add extra compiler and linkers arguments in the Cython magic.
+
+  .. code-block:: python
+
+         # For GCC and for clang
+         %%cython --compile-args=-fopenmp --link-args=-fopenmp
+         # For Microsoft's compilers
+         %%cython --compile-args=/openmp --link-args=/openmp
+
+* To debug C code (e.g. a segfault), do use ``gdb`` with:
+
+  .. code-block:: bash
+
+         gdb --ex r --args python ./entrypoint_to_bug_reproducer.py
+
+* To have access to some value in place to debug in ``cdef (nogil)`` context, use:
+
+  .. code-block:: cython
+
+         with gil:
+             print(state_to_print)
+
+* Note that Cython cannot parse f-strings with ``{var=}`` expressions, e.g.
+
+  .. code-block:: bash
+
+         print(f"{test_val=}")
+
+* scikit-learn codebase has a lot of non-unified (fused) types (re)definitions.
+  There currently is `ongoing work to simplify and unify that across the codebase
+  <https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/25572>`_.
+  For now, make sure you understand which concrete types are used ultimately.
+
+* You might find this alias to compile individual Cython extension handy:
+
+    .. code-block::
+
+         # You might want to add this alias to your shell script config.
+         alias cythonX="cython -X language_level=3 -X boundscheck=False -X wraparound=False -X initializedcheck=False -X nonecheck=False -X cdivision=True"
+
+         # This generates `source.c` as as if you had recompiled scikit-learn entirely.
+         cythonX --annotate source.pyx
+
+* Using the ``--annotate`` option with this flag allows generating a HTML report of code annotation.
+  This report indicates interactions with the CPython interpreter on a line-by-line basis.
+  Interactions with the CPython interpreter must be avoided as much as possible in
+  the computationally intensive sections of the algorithms.
+  For more information, please refer to `this section of Cython's tutorial <https://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/src/tutorial/cython_tutorial.html#primes>`_
+
+    .. code-block::
+
+         # This generates a HTML report (`source.html`) for `source.c`.
+         cythonX --annotate source.pyx
+
+Tips for performance
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+* Understand the GIL in context for CPython (which problems it solves, what are its limitations)
+  and get a good understanding of when Cython will be mapped to C code free of interactions with
+  CPython, when it will not, and when it cannot (e.g. presence of interactions with Python
+  objects, which include functions). In this regard, `PEP073 <https://peps.python.org/pep-0703/>`_
+  provides a good overview and context and pathways for removal.
+
+* Make sure you have deactivated `checks <https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/62a017efa047e9581ae7df8bbaa62cf4c0544ee4/sklearn/_build_utils/__init__.py#L68-L87>`_.
+
+* Always prefer memoryviews instead over ``cnp.ndarray`` when possible: memoryviews are lightweight.
+
+* Avoid memoryview slicing: memoryview slicing might be costly or misleading in some cases and","path":"doc/developers/cython.rst","commit_id":"530ec85a8a4345b3109214ced9e50312e2f9be9b","original_commit_id":"530ec85a8a4345b3109214ced9e50312e2f9be9b","user":{"login":"da-woods","id":10536947,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNTM2OTQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10536947?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods","html_url":"https://github.com/da-woods","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"FWIW I have an PR that should dramatically improve this. Reference Issues/PRs
Discussed a little [here](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/25561#issuecomment-1620598121)

#### What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.
The `{r}dist_csr` methods now accept pointers rather than memory views since none of the peripheral data of memory views are used. This significantly decreases call overhead, which is especially beneficial for the tight loops in which these functions are often used.

This also includes a formatting fix for `HaversineDistance`

#### Any other comments?

I suspect practically they are contiguous in practice, but it might be nice to enforce that.

Passing a pointer is about as light-weight as you can get, so nothing Cython can do is ever likely to beat it. The changes I mentioned in the linked issue should bring memoryview slicing a lot closer, but pointers are still likely to win. (Essentially all I've done is noticed that taking multiple slices of the same array is a reference counting no-op, so you pay for one reference count at the start of the loop and that's it)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/comments/1625690496/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Reference Issues/PRs
Discussed a little [here](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/25561#issuecomment-1620598121)

#### What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.
The `{r}dist_csr` methods now accept pointers rather than memory views since none of the peripheral data of memory views are used. This significantly decreases call overhead, which is especially beneficial for the tight loops in which these functions are often used.

This also includes a formatting fix for `HaversineDistance`

#### Any other comments?

Reference issue
Fixes gh-18716.

#### What does this implement/fix?
I added fused type signatures for breadth-first + depth-first traversals, along with connected components. This allows the existing API to support sparse arrays/matrices with int64 index dtypes. After gh-18509, this situation is more likely to occur in user code.

#### Additional information
You may notice that the body of each modified function is unchanged by this PR. This means that matrices large enough to actually need int64 index arrays will still not work properly, but those were unsupported before anyway.

The introduction of fused dtypes increases the shared library size (not surprisingly). On my machine, the `_traversal.cpython-311-x86_64-linux-gnu.so` is currently 554K on disk, and with this PR it grows to 2.3M. A nontrivial portion of this comes from the way we treat the undirected code paths, as the `.transpose().tocsr()` operation doesn't guarantee that the index dtype of the result will be the same as the original matrix.

*Cython note*: I needed to add extra `def` functions for each of the `cdef` functions that I modified, otherwise the Cython compiler would complain to me about "no suitable method found" and "Invalid use of fused types, type cannot be specialized". I don't fully understand why adding the indirection solves the problem, but it seems okay for now.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/18913/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/18913/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/1646777949","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/18913#issuecomment-1646777949","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/18913","id":1646777949,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc5iJ9Zd","user":{"login":"da-woods","id":10536947,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNTM2OTQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10536947?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods","html_url":"https://github.com/da-woods","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-07-23T08:18:24Z","updated_at":"2023-07-23T08:18:24Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> @da-woods: do you know why functions can't be resolved here except if associated `def`-front-end are defined?

So the `def` function has a runtime resolution mechanism while the `cdef` function has to be resolved at compile time.

Just looking at one use of it

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/faf06dca7327f9cde7424c30124631af5a0bff3a/scipy/sparse/csgraph/_traversal.pyx#L344

you're calling it with untyped variables so that it has to use the runtime resolution.

I suspect `cpdef` would also work here (but I haven't tested it). Reference issue
Fixes gh-18716.

#### What does this implement/fix?
I added fused type signatures for breadth-first + depth-first traversals, along with connected components. This allows the existing API to support sparse arrays/matrices with int64 index dtypes. After gh-18509, this situation is more likely to occur in user code.

#### Additional information
You may notice that the body of each modified function is unchanged by this PR. This means that matrices large enough to actually need int64 index arrays will still not work properly, but those were unsupported before anyway.

The introduction of fused dtypes increases the shared library size (not surprisingly). On my machine, the `_traversal.cpython-311-x86_64-linux-gnu.so` is currently 554K on disk, and with this PR it grows to 2.3M. A nontrivial portion of this comes from the way we treat the undirected code paths, as the `.transpose().tocsr()` operation doesn't guarantee that the index dtype of the result will be the same as the original matrix.

*Cython note*: I needed to add extra `def` functions for each of the `cdef` functions that I modified, otherwise the Cython compiler would complain to me about "no suitable method found" and "Invalid use of fused types, type cannot be specialized".     # ... then wrap the functions yourself
```

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pulls/comments/1125485662","pull_request_review_id":1325052674,"id":1125485662,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAAzd1s5DFYxe","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,178 @@
+.. _cython:
+
+Cython Best Practices, Conventions and Knowledge
+================================================
+
+This documents:
+
+* technical aspects of Cython which aren't currently present in Cython's documentation
+* tips to develop Cython code in scikit-learn
+
+Cython Internals
+----------------
+
+Concrete method dispatch implementation
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+* In most cases, concrete method dispatch is performed **at runtime using virtual tables**
+  (i.e. static C-array of pointers to a given types of function, used at runtime to known which
+  function to call from an instance). This is costly because there is a double indirection.
+
+* In some cases, concrete method dispatch is performed **statically** (a function is called
+  directly by a pointer dereference). This is less costly, especially if there are rooms
+  for this function to be inline.
+
+Known Limitations
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+* No template for types exist in Cython, alternatives are:
+
+  * using fused-types
+  * using Tempita, a small preprocessing language to expand Cython source
+
+* Multiple inheritance is not possible for extension types
+* No concept similar to interfaces or traits exist in Cython
+* Currently it is impossible to inherit and override a method which has a fused type in its signature
+* Structs cannot be created using constructor syntax with `nogil` `issue and workaround <https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/1642>`_
+
+Performances improvements will come with `Cython 3.0 which is to be released soon in beta apparently <https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/4022#issuecomment-1340918590>`_
+
+Basics
+^^^^^^
+
+* ``nogil`` declaration: https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/2798#issuecomment-459971828
+
+Tips for developping Cython code in scikit-learn
+------------------------------------------------
+
+Tips to ease development
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+* If you intend to use OpenMP: On MacOS, system's distribution of ``clang`` does not implement OpenMP.
+  You can install the ``compilers`` package available on ``conda-forge`` which comes with implementations of OpenMP.
+
+* Activating `checks <https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/62a017efa047e9581ae7df8bbaa62cf4c0544ee4/sklearn/_build_utils/__init__.py#L68-L87>`_ might help. E.g. for activating boundscheck use:
+
+  .. code-block:: bash
+
+         export SKLEARN_ENABLE_DEBUG_CYTHON_DIRECTIVES=1
+
+* Start from scratch in a notebook to understand.
+* To debug C code (e.g. a segfault), do use ``gdb`` with:
+
+  .. code-block:: bash
+
+         gdb --ex r --args python ./entrypoint_to_bug_reproducer.py
+
+* To have access to some value in place to debug in ``cdef (nogil)`` context, use:
+
+  .. code-block:: cython
+
+         with gil:
+             print(state_to_print)
+
+* Note that Cython cannot parse f-strings with ``{var=}`` expressions, e.g.
+    ``print(f"{test_val=}")``
+
+* scikit-learn codebase is a bit of a zero of (fused) types (re)definitions, and we need to simplified
+  and unify that across the codebase. For now, make sure you understand which concrete types are used ultimately.
+
+* It is helpful to use ``gdb`` to debug. In order to do so, one must use a Python interpreter built with debug support
+  (debug symbols and proper optimization). To create a new conda environment with a source-built interpreter:
+
+  .. code-block:: bash
+
+         git clone https://github.com/python/cpython.git
+         conda create -n debug-scikit-dev
+         conda activate debug-scikit-dev
+         cd cpython
+         mkdir debug
+         cd debug
+         ../configure --prefix=$CONDA_PREFIX --with-pydebug
+         make EXTRA_CFLAGS='-DPy_DEBUG' -j<num_cores>
+         make install
+
+* You may need to restart conda after building/installing (I had to, but not sure why).
+
+
+  * You might find this alias handy:
+    .. code-block::
+
+         alias cythonX="cython -X language_level=3 -X boundscheck=False -X wraparound=False -X initializedcheck=False -X nonecheck=False -X cdivision=True"
+
+  * which you can use with:
+
+    .. code-block::
+
+         cythonX --annotate source.pyx
+
+Tips for performances
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+
+* Understand the GIL in context for CPython (which problems it solves, what are its limitations) and get a good
+  understanding of when Cython will be mapped to C code free of interactions with CPython, when it will not, and when
+  it cannot (e.g. presence of interactions with Python objects, which include functions).
+  In this regard, `PEP073 <https://peps.python.org/pep-0703/>`_ provides a good overview and context and pathways for removal.
+* Make sure you have deactivated `checks <https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/62a017efa047e9581ae7df8bbaa62cf4c0544ee4/sklearn/_build_utils/__init__.py#L68-L87>`_.
+* Always prefer memoryviews instead over ``cnp.ndarray`` when possible: memoryview are lightweight.
+* Avoid memoryview slicing: memoryview slicing might be costly or misleading in some cases and we better not use it IMO,
+  even if handling fewer dimensions in some context would be preferable.","path":"doc/developers/cython.rst","position":93,"original_position":120,"commit_id":"142e3519f44deeb47154ad472cfe8345ca4042e4","original_commit_id":"5b4f9ffbc801f9ace30565b27836ce69481af63e","user":{"login":"da-woods","id":10536947,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNTM2OTQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10536947?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods","html_url":"https://github.com/da-woods","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"There's definitely some overhead. It's relatively lightweight compared to (say) creating a new memoryview, and it can be done without the GIL. But it isn't free.

I assume this based on genuine hard-earned experience within Pandas.

There's also the general rule that "user code should aim to be clean and understandable, while library code might want to squeeze out every little bit of performance". I assume Pandas is more like library code in this case, so my recommendation would be a bit different than to the average user.

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pulls/comments/1125491039","pull_request_review_id":1325060081,"id":1125491039,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAAzd1s5DFaFf","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,178 @@
+.. _cython:
+
+Cython Best Practices, Conventions and Knowledge
+================================================
+
+This documents:
+
+* technical aspects of Cython which aren't currently present in Cython's documentation
+* tips to develop Cython code in scikit-learn
+
+Cython Internals
+----------------
+
+Concrete method dispatch implementation
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+* In most cases, concrete method dispatch is performed **at runtime using virtual tables**
+  (i.e. static C-array of pointers to a given types of function, used at runtime to known which
+  function to call from an instance). This is costly because there is a double indirection.
+
+* In some cases, concrete method dispatch is performed **statically** (a function is called
+  directly by a pointer dereference). This is less costly, especially if there are rooms
+  for this function to be inline.
+
+Known Limitations
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+* No template for types exist in Cython, alternatives are:
+
+  * using fused-types
+  * using Tempita, a small preprocessing language to expand Cython source
+
+* Multiple inheritance is not possible for extension types
+* No concept similar to interfaces or traits exist in Cython
+* Currently it is impossible to inherit and override a method which has a fused type in its signature
+* Structs cannot be created using constructor syntax with `nogil` `issue and workaround <https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/1642>`_
+
+Performances improvements will come with `Cython 3.0 which is to be released soon in beta apparently <https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/4022#issuecomment-1340918590>`_
+
+Basics
+^^^^^^
+
+* ``nogil`` declaration: https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/2798#issuecomment-459971828
+
+Tips for developping Cython code in scikit-learn
+------------------------------------------------
+
+Tips to ease development
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+* If you intend to use OpenMP: On MacOS, system's distribution of ``clang`` does not implement OpenMP.
+  You can install the ``compilers`` package available on ``conda-forge`` which comes with implementations of OpenMP.
+
+* Activating `checks <https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/62a017efa047e9581ae7df8bbaa62cf4c0544ee4/sklearn/_build_utils/__init__.py#L68-L87>`_ might help. E.g. for activating boundscheck use:
+
+  .. code-block:: bash
+
+         export SKLEARN_ENABLE_DEBUG_CYTHON_DIRECTIVES=1
+
+* Start from scratch in a notebook to understand.
+* To debug C code (e.g. a segfault), do use ``gdb`` with:
+
+  .. code-block:: bash
+
+         gdb --ex r --args python ./entrypoint_to_bug_reproducer.py
+
+* To have access to some value in place to debug in ``cdef (nogil)`` context, use:
+
+  .. code-block:: cython
+
+         with gil:
+             print(state_to_print)
+
+* Note that Cython cannot parse f-strings with ``{var=}`` expressions, e.g.
+    ``print(f"{test_val=}")``
+
+* scikit-learn codebase is a bit of a zero of (fused) types (re)definitions, and we need to simplified
+  and unify that across the codebase. For now, make sure you understand which concrete types are used ultimately.
+
+* It is helpful to use ``gdb`` to debug. In order to do so, one must use a Python interpreter built with debug support
+  (debug symbols and proper optimization). To create a new conda environment with a source-built interpreter:
+
+  .. code-block:: bash
+
+         git clone https://github.com/python/cpython.git
+         conda create -n debug-scikit-dev
+         conda activate debug-scikit-dev
+         cd cpython
+         mkdir debug
+         cd debug
+         ../configure --prefix=$CONDA_PREFIX --with-pydebug
+         make EXTRA_CFLAGS='-DPy_DEBUG' -j<num_cores>
+         make install
+
+* You may need to restart conda after building/installing (I had to, but not sure why).
+
+
+  * You might find this alias handy:
+    .. code-block::
+
+         alias cythonX="cython -X language_level=3 -X boundscheck=False -X wraparound=False -X initializedcheck=False -X nonecheck=False -X cdivision=True"
+
+  * which you can use with:
+
+    .. code-block::
+
+         cythonX --annotate source.pyx","path":"doc/developers/cython.rst","position":78,"original_position":107,"commit_id":"142e3519f44deeb47154ad472cfe8345ca4042e4","original_commit_id":"5b4f9ffbc801f9ace30565b27836ce69481af63e","user":{"login":"da-woods","id":10536947,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNTM2OTQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10536947?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods","html_url":"https://github.com/da-woods","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"There's definitely some examples of annotation in the Cython documentation (https://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/src/tutorial/cython_tutorial.html#primes). #### Reference Issues/PRs
<!--
Example: Fixes #1234. See also #3456.
Please use keywords (e.g., Fixes) to create link to the issues or pull requests
you resolved, so that they will automatically be closed when your pull request
is merged. See https://github.com/blog/1506-closing-issues-via-pull-requests
-->
Fixes sklearn/utils/_random.pyx from #[25484](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/25484)


#### What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.

- Replaces cnp.ndarray with memory views in sklearn/utils/_random.pyx


#### Any other comments?

The output needed to be casted to an numpy array as tests (for example test_svm.py) call .astype on the return of the modified functions.

I also tried to reason as to why cnp.int_t and int are the types as values are intuitively indices. The indices can be between 0<= index < inf. Should we be using an unsigned long maybe? So, a cnp.uint64_t and np.uint64 or maybe a uint32.

+.. _cython:
+
+Cython Best Practices, Conventions and Knowledge
+================================================
+
+This documents:
+
+* technical aspects of Cython which aren't currently present in Cython's documentation
+* tips to develop Cython code in scikit-learn
+
+Cython Internals
+----------------
+
+Concrete method dispatch implementation
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+* In most cases, concrete method dispatch is performed **at runtime using virtual tables**
+  (i.e. static C-array of pointers to a given types of function, used at runtime to known which
+  function to call from an instance). This is costly because there is a double indirection.
+
+* In some cases, concrete method dispatch is performed **statically** (a function is called
+  directly by a pointer dereference). This is less costly, especially if there are rooms
+  for this function to be inline.
+
+Known Limitations
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+* No template for types exist in Cython, alternatives are:
+
+  * using fused-types
+  * using Tempita, a small preprocessing language to expand Cython source
+
+* Multiple inheritance is not possible for extension types
+* No concept similar to interfaces or traits exist in Cython
+* Currently it is impossible to inherit and override a method which has a fused type in its signature
+* Structs cannot be created using constructor syntax with `nogil` `issue and workaround <https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/1642>`_
+
+Performances improvements will come with `Cython 3.0 which is to be released soon in beta apparently <https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/4022#issuecomment-1340918590>`_
+
+Basics
+^^^^^^
+
+* ``nogil`` declaration: https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/2798#issuecomment-459971828
+
+Tips for developping Cython code in scikit-learn
+------------------------------------------------
+
+Tips to ease development
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+* If you intend to use OpenMP: On MacOS, system's distribution of ``clang`` does not implement OpenMP.
+  You can install the ``compilers`` package available on ``conda-forge`` which comes with implementations of OpenMP.
+
+* Activating `checks <https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/62a017efa047e9581ae7df8bbaa62cf4c0544ee4/sklearn/_build_utils/__init__.py#L68-L87>`_ might help. E.g. for activating boundscheck use:
+
+  .. code-block:: bash
+
+         export SKLEARN_ENABLE_DEBUG_CYTHON_DIRECTIVES=1
+
+* Start from scratch in a notebook to understand.
+* To debug C code (e.g. a segfault), do use ``gdb`` with:
+
+  .. code-block:: bash
+
+         gdb --ex r --args python ./entrypoint_to_bug_reproducer.py
+
+* To have access to some value in place to debug in ``cdef (nogil)`` context, use:
+
+  .. code-block:: cython
+
+         with gil:
+             print(state_to_print)
+
+* Note that Cython cannot parse f-strings with ``{var=}`` expressions, e.g.
+    ``print(f"{test_val=}")``","path":"doc/developers/cython.rst","position":75,"original_position":75,"commit_id":"3fbb4afee9f1ec067065d99b521fc500f567ec8e","original_commit_id":"3fbb4afee9f1ec067065d99b521fc500f567ec8e","user":{"login":"da-woods","id":10536947,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNTM2OTQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10536947?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods","html_url":"https://github.com/da-woods","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This is in Cython 3 I believe. +.. _cython:
+
+Cython Best Practices, Conventions and Knowledge
+================================================
+
+This documents:
+
+* technical aspects of Cython which aren't currently present in Cython's documentation
+* tips to develop Cython code in scikit-learn
+
+Cython Internals
+----------------
+
+Concrete method dispatch implementation
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+* In most cases, concrete method dispatch is performed **at runtime using virtual tables**
+  (i.e. static C-array of pointers to a given types of function, used at runtime to known which
+  function to call from an instance). This is costly because there is a double indirection.
+
+* In some cases, concrete method dispatch is performed **statically** (a function is called
+  directly by a pointer dereference). This is less costly, especially if there are rooms
+  for this function to be inline.
+
+Known Limitations
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+* No template for types exist in Cython, alternatives are:
+
+  * using fused-types
+  * using Tempita, a small preprocessing language to expand Cython source
+
+* Multiple inheritance is not possible for extension types
+* No concept similar to interfaces or traits exist in Cython
+* Currently it is impossible to inherit and override a method which has a fused type in its signature
+* Structs cannot be created using constructor syntax with `nogil` `issue and workaround <https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/1642>`_
+
+Performances improvements will come with `Cython 3.0 which is to be released soon in beta apparently <https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/4022#issuecomment-1340918590>`_
+
+Basics
+^^^^^^
+
+* ``nogil`` declaration: https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/2798#issuecomment-459971828
+
+Tips for developping Cython code in scikit-learn
+------------------------------------------------
+
+Tips to ease development
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+* If you intend to use OpenMP: On MacOS, system's distribution of ``clang`` does not implement OpenMP.
+  You can install the ``compilers`` package available on ``conda-forge`` which comes with implementations of OpenMP.
+
+* Activating `checks <https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/62a017efa047e9581ae7df8bbaa62cf4c0544ee4/sklearn/_build_utils/__init__.py#L68-L87>`_ might help. E.g. for activating boundscheck use:
+
+  .. code-block:: bash
+
+         export SKLEARN_ENABLE_DEBUG_CYTHON_DIRECTIVES=1
+
+* Start from scratch in a notebook to understand.
+* To debug C code (e.g. a segfault), do use ``gdb`` with:
+
+  .. code-block:: bash
+
+         gdb --ex r --args python ./entrypoint_to_bug_reproducer.py
+
+* To have access to some value in place to debug in ``cdef (nogil)`` context, use:
+
+  .. code-block:: cython
+
+         with gil:
+             print(state_to_print)
+
+* Note that Cython cannot parse f-strings with ``{var=}`` expressions, e.g.
+    ``print(f"{test_val=}")``
+
+* scikit-learn codebase is a bit of a zero of (fused) types (re)definitions, and we need to simplified
+  and unify that across the codebase. For now, make sure you understand which concrete types are used ultimately.
+
+* It is helpful to use ``gdb`` to debug. In order to do so, one must use a Python interpreter built with debug support
+  (debug symbols and proper optimization). To create a new conda environment with a source-built interpreter:
+
+  .. code-block:: bash
+
+         git clone https://github.com/python/cpython.git
+         conda create -n debug-scikit-dev
+         conda activate debug-scikit-dev
+         cd cpython
+         mkdir debug
+         cd debug
+         ../configure --prefix=$CONDA_PREFIX --with-pydebug
+         make EXTRA_CFLAGS='-DPy_DEBUG' -j<num_cores>
+         make install
+
+* You may need to restart conda after building/installing (I had to, but not sure why).
+
+
+  * You might find this alias handy:
+    .. code-block::
+
+         alias cythonX="cython -X language_level=3 -X boundscheck=False -X wraparound=False -X initializedcheck=False -X nonecheck=False -X cdivision=True"
+
+  * which you can use with:
+
+    .. code-block::
+
+         cythonX --annotate source.pyx
+
+Tips for performances
+^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
+
+
+* Understand the GIL in context for CPython (which problems it solves, what are its limitations) and get a good
+  understanding of when Cython will be mapped to C code free of interactions with CPython, when it will not, and when
+  it cannot (e.g. presence of interactions with Python objects, which include functions).
+  In this regard, `PEP073 <https://peps.python.org/pep-0703/>`_ provides a good overview and context and pathways for removal.
+* Make sure you have deactivated `checks <https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/62a017efa047e9581ae7df8bbaa62cf4c0544ee4/sklearn/_build_utils/__init__.py#L68-L87>`_.
+* Always prefer memoryviews instead over ``cnp.ndarray`` when possible: memoryview are lightweight.
+* Avoid memoryview slicing: memoryview slicing might be costly or misleading in some cases and we better not use it IMO,
+  even if handling fewer dimensions in some context would be preferable.
+* Decorate final classes or methods with ``@final`` (this allows removing virtual tables when needed)
+* Inline methods and function when it makes sense
+* Make sure your Cython compilation units `use NumPy recent C API <https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/62a017efa047e9581ae7df8bbaa62cf4c0544ee4/setup.py#L64-L70>`_.
+* In doubt, read the generated C or C++ code if you can: "The fewer C instructions and indirections for a line of Cython code, the better" is a good rule of thumb.","path":"doc/developers/cython.rst","position":124,"original_position":124,"commit_id":"3fbb4afee9f1ec067065d99b521fc500f567ec8e","original_commit_id":"3fbb4afee9f1ec067065d99b521fc500f567ec8e","user":{"login":"da-woods","id":10536947,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNTM2OTQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/10536947?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods","html_url":"https://github.com/da-woods","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/da-woods/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This is a good point.
